coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of photoluminescent glycodendrimer with terminal β-cyclodextrin molecules as a biocompatible pH-sensitive carrier for doxorubicin delivery
coredata.dc:creator: Pooresmaeil, Malihe
Namazi, Hassan
Salehi, Roya
coredata.dc:description: In the present research, we prepared new glycodendrimer containing β-cyclodextrin (β-CD) in three steps. At first, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) synthesized through pyrolysis of the citric acid (CA). Then the polyamidoamine (PAMAM) dendrimer was grown from the surface of the modified GQDs (GQDs-PAMAM). Finally, the prepared GQDs-PAMAM was functionalized with β-CD to obtain the glycodendrimer (GQDs-PAMAM-β-CD). The synthesized glycodendrimer characterized using several techniques. The phenol-sulfuric acid test obtained the amount of the β-CD about 30.37 %. 61.2 % of doxorubicin (DOX) was loaded in the prepared glycodendrimer. DOX@GQDs-PAMAM-β-CD displayed the pH-sensitive drug release profile, which fitted the Higuchi kinetic model. The biological test outcomes showed that the GQDs-PAMAM-β-CD is a safe carrier with good capability in penetration to the cancer cells. Moreover, DOX@GQDs-PAMAM-β-CD exhibited more efficiency in the killing of the cancer cells compared to neat DOX. Obtained results suggested that prepared glycodendrimer could be a potential nanosystem for breast cancer treatment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: β-cyclodextrin
Graphene quantum dots
Polyamidoamine
Glycodendrimer
Nanocarrier
Monitoring of drug release

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Growth of the PAMAM from the surface of the modified GQDs (GQDs-PAMAM)
Calculation the amount of the grafted β-CD to the GQDs-PAMAM
In vitro DOX load and release study
Study of the drug release mechanism
In vitro drug load and release studies

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Authors gratefully acknowledge the University of Tabriz (grant number 948645103 ) and Research Center for Pharmaceutical Nanotechnology (RCPN) of the Tabriz University of Medical Science for the financial supports of this research.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FTIR spectra of GQDs (a), GQDs-NH2 (b), GQDs-PAMAM-G1 (c), GQDs-PAMAM-G2 (d), GQDs-PAMAM-G3 (e), mono-6-OTs-β-CD (f), and GQDs-PAMAM-β-CD (g).

The obtained zeta potential values for the prepared samples (a), absorption spectra of the product of GQDs-PAMAM-β-CD reaction with phenol/sulfuric acid at the absorbance range of 350-600 nm (b), Nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms and BJH (inset) plot of GQDs-PAMAM-β-CD (c) and the obtained PL spectra for GQDs and GQDs-PAMAM-β-CD (d).
SEM images of GQDs (a), GQDs-PAMAM-β-CD (b), their respective size distributions histogram (c and d).
The AFM images and the corresponding height profile of the prepared GQDs (a) and GQDs-PAMAM-β-CD (b).
TEM images of GQDs (a) and GQDs-PAMAM-β-CD (b).

The cumulative DOX release profile from DOX@GQDs-PAMAM-β-CD (a), the percentage of the viability for the treated MDA-MB 231 cells with GQDs-PAMAM and GQDs-PAMAM-β-CD (b) free DOX, and DOX@GQDs-PAMAM-β-CD (c), and obtained IC50 values. Data are expressed as the mean ± standard deviation (n = 3). In the MTT test, the untreated cells were considered as control.

Uptake to the MDA-MB 231 cells; the fluorescent microscopy images of rhodamine B-labeled GQDs-PAMAM-β-CD (a) and DOX@GQDs-PAMAM-β-CD (b), as well as, flow-cytometry results for GQDs-PAMAM-β-CD (c), DOX@GQDs-PAMAM-β-CD (d), and control (e).
The assessment of the nuclear morphology using DAPI staining nuclei of the cells (MDA-MB 231) after 48 h exposure to control (a), GQDs-PAMAM-β-CD (b), DOX (c), and DOX@GQDs-PAMAM-β-CD (d).
The cells distribution in different phases of the cell cycle after 48 h treatment with control (a), GQDs-PAMAM-β-CD (b), DOX (c), and DOX@GQDs-PAMAM-β-CD (d).
Schematic illustration of the GQDs-PAMAM glycodendrimer synthesis.
DOX release kinetic parameters in two different releases medium.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of photoluminescent glycodendrimer with terminal β-cyclodextrin molecules as a biocompatible pH-sensitive carrier for doxorubicin delivery

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Laboratory of Dendrimers and Nano-Biopolymers, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, P.O. Box 51666, Tabriz, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Laboratory of Dendrimers and Nano-Biopolymers

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Research Laboratory of Dendrimers and Nano-Biopolymers, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, P.O. Box 51666, Tabriz, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Center for Pharmaceutical Nanotechnology (RCPN), Tabriz University of Medical Science, Tabriz, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Research Center for Pharmaceutical Nanotechnology (RCPN), Tabriz University of Medical Science, Tabriz, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Drug Applied Research Center, and Department of Medical Nanotechnology, Department of Advanced Medical Science, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Drug Applied Research Center, and Department of Medical Nanotechnology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Drug Applied Research Center, Department of Medical Nanotechnology, Tabriz, Iran

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Research Laboratory of Dendrimers and Nano-Biopolymers, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, P.O. Box 51666, Tabriz, Iran.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Laboratory of Dendrimers and Nano-Biopolymers

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the present research, we prepared new glycodendrimer containing β-cyclodextrin (β-CD) in three steps. At first, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) synthesized through pyrolysis of the citric acid (CA). Then the polyamidoamine (PAMAM) dendrimer was grown from the surface of the modified GQDs (GQDs-PAMAM). Finally, the prepared GQDs-PAMAM was functionalized with β-CD to obtain the glycodendrimer (GQDs-PAMAM-β-CD). The synthesized glycodendrimer characterized using several techniques. The phenol-sulfuric acid test obtained the amount of the β-CD about 30.37 %. 61.2 % of doxorubicin (DOX) was loaded in the prepared glycodendrimer. DOX@GQDs-PAMAM-β-CD displayed the pH-sensitive drug release profile, which fitted the Higuchi kinetic model. The biological test outcomes showed that the GQDs-PAMAM-β-CD is a safe carrier with good capability in penetration to the cancer cells. Moreover, DOX@GQDs-PAMAM-β-CD exhibited more efficiency in the killing of the cancer cells compared to neat DOX. Obtained results suggested that prepared glycodendrimer could be a potential nanosystem for breast cancer treatment.

ce:section.ce:para: Over the last decades, cancer treatment has become a main issue in the world. Chemotherapy combination with surgery is the usually used way for cancer treatment. The chemotherapy drugs can kill the cancer cells due to their highly toxic properties. However, low water solubility, low bioavailability, and low local therapeutic concentration are some of the side effects of the cancer treatment by using the chemotherapy drugs, as well as, they are commonly not able to distinguish between the normal and cancer cells (Javanbakht, Pooresmaeil, & Namazi, 2019; Liu et al., 2017; Namazi & Belali, 2016; Pooresmaeil, Behzadi Nia, & Namazi, 2019; Pooresmaeil & Namazi, 2018a; Tian, Hua, & Liu, 2020).

Todays, GQDs with their exceptional properties attracted great deals of attention in various areas such as drug delivery and bioimaging. It is worth to mention that the produced cytotoxicity from the metallic QDs limits their usage in biology. Therefore, the appeal for the biocompatible imaging agents led to the synthesis of the carbon-based fluorescent NPs (Javanbakht, Nazari, Rakhshaei, & Namazi, 2018). These materials are a new emerging class of the graphene family with a zero-dimensional structure having single-or few-layer graphene, luminescence property, and bandgap tuning abilities. GQDs possess the properties of both graphene nature compounds and quantum dots (Ettefaghi et al., 2017; Hashemzadeh et al., 2016). Pyrolysis of CA is the common economical technique for the synthesis of GQDs. This biomaterial found in most of the animals and plants, especially in citrus fruits. However, mainly it produced by fermentation of glucose or starch. The large surface area, great inherent fluorescence, more biocompatibility, good photostability, more oxygen-containing functional groups, and rich in delocalized π electrons are some advantages of the GQDs (Dong et al., 2018; Javanbakht & Namazi, 2018). The presence of the oxygen-containing functional groups in the GQDs structure offers its modification possibility for obtaining the new drug carrier with both advantages of the GQDs and a modifying agent such as polymers, dendrimers, biological molecules, and targeting ligands (Bansal et al., 2019).

Dendrimers are a new category of three-dimensional polymeric materials. These classes of highly branched macromolecules have monodisperse three-dimensional architecture and high functionality (Hosseyni, Pooresmaeil, & Namazi, 2020; Namazi et al., 2007; Namazi, Hashemipour, & Toomari, 2017; Namazi & Jafarirad, 2011; Toomari, Namazi, & Akbar, 2015). Because of their special architecture, highly-defined structure, abundant functional groups on the outer surface, high surface areas, and numerous cavities in their structure, dendritic macromolecules exhibited a high capacity for encapsulation of drug molecules, hence, they are a good candidate for drug delivery (Li et al., 2015; Namazi, Heydari, & Pourfarzolla, 2014; Namazi, Mohammad Pour Fard, & Pooresmaeil, 2019; Namazi & Toomari Hamrahloo, 2011; Pooresmaeil & Namazi, 2019). The end functional groups of dendrimers could be reacted with other molecules to form the new dendrimers with the improved property.

β-CD is one of the biocompatible carbohydrates which could be used for upgrading the biocompatibility and some of the other property of the drug delivery systems (Mehraz, Nouri, & Namazi, 2020; Namazi & Heydari, 2014; Pooresmaeil & Namazi, 2019). Todays, β-CD with a torus-shaped structure comprising from the glucopyranose units attracted substantial interest in biomedicine because of its enzymatic hydrolysis possibility. The outer surface of this molecule is hydrophilic (COH groups) but its inner cavity is hydrophobic (COC and C–H bonds) (Namazi, Bahrami, & Entezami, 2005; Namazi, Fathi, & Heydari, 2012; Namazi et al., 2014). β-CD could be covalently attached to the dendrimers and improved their features from the viewpoint of biocompatibility and drug loading (%). Attaching of the β-CD to the dendrimers produced the glycodendrimers with both advantages of β-CD and common dendrimer. The unique structure of the glycodendrimer containing β-CD provides the possibility of loading a wide range of drugs; both hydrophobic and hydrophilic drugs with optimum drug loading capacity (Hernandez-Montelongo et al., 2014; Shahiwala, 2019).

Up to now, numerous studies were performed to evaluate the ability of the PAMAM dendrimer for DOX delivery. However, our survey on the literature showed that, until today, no work has been published on the synthesis of GQDs-PAMAM-β-CD as a carrier for DOX delivery. In fact, the key objective of the present investigation is introducing the more biocompatible and biodegradable β-CD groups as an oligosaccharide on the carrier structure and assess its effect on drug loading and biocompatibility of the system. For this goal, the synthesized GQDs were modified with 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTS). With divergent synthesis, the layer by layer growth of PAMAM dendrimers formed GQDs-PAMAM. The GQDs-PAMAM functionalized with β-CD to improve the efficiency and biocompatibility of the prepared system. The prepared system was characterized by several techniques; Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), atomic force microscopy (AFM), Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET), and zeta potential analysis. GQDs-PAMAM-β-CD showed 61.2 % of DOX loading and controlled drug release behavior. The biological tests approved the non-toxicity of the GQDs-PAMAM-β-CD and its high efficiency for cancer therapy after DOX loading.
The used materials in this work are expressed in part 1.1 of the Electronic Supplementary Information (ESI).
The details of the characterization techniques and any information related to the used devices can found in part 1.2 of ESI.

As previously reported (Dong et al., 2012), GQDs were prepared from the direct pyrolysis of the CA. In this regard, CA (2 g) was poured in a Pyrex beaker and then placed in a heating mantle at 200 °C. After 30 min, the heating process produced the orange liquid from the white powder of CA. The prepared orange liquid of GQDs dropped into the NaOH (10 mg/mL) to achieve the neutralized GQDs. The aqueous solution of the obtained GQDs removed through evaporation with rotary under the reduced pressure. Finally, dry GQDs obtained after heating in a vacuum oven and kept in a vial for further use.

For the preparation of the amine-functionalized GQDs, under the argon atmosphere, the 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTS) solution (2.25 mL of APTS in 10 mL dry toluene) was added drop by drop to the mixture of the dispersed GQDs (0.5 g) in dry toluene (40 mL). After 24 h reflux, the prepared GQDs-NH2 was collected with centrifuging at 10,000 rpm and washed with toluene and then with ethanol. The pure GQDs-NH2 obtained after several washing and collection operations. In the end, GQDs-NH2 was dried in a vacuum oven at 70 °C (Nasseri, Allahresani, & Raissi, 2014).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Growth of the PAMAM from the surface of the modified GQDs (GQDs-PAMAM)
ce:section.ce:para: The GQDs-PAMAM-G1 was synthesized in two steps method and according to (Ma, Xing, Shao, Du, & La, 2017; Zarghami, Akbari, Latifi, & Amani, 2016) with some modification; Michael addition reaction of methyl acrylate (MA) with amino groups of the GQDs-NH2 (1), and then the amidation reaction of the ethylenediamine (EDA) with terminal methyl ester groups (2).

The Michael addition reaction of the MA with amino groups of the GQDs-NH2 was carried out as follows: Finely dispersed GQDs-NH2 was prepared through the sonication of GQDs-NH2 in the anhydrous methanol. The reaction temperature was set at 50 °C, and then the MA slowly was added into the above reaction mixture under the argon atmosphere. The reaction mixture stirred continuously for 18 h in this condition. In the following, the prepared GQDs-PAMAM-G0.5 collected by using a centrifuge at 10,000 rpm. The high pure of GQDs-PAMAM-G0.5 was achieved via a repeated washing with anhydrous ethanol and collecting with a centrifuge. In the end, the resulting powder was dried in a vacuum oven for 24 h at 50 °C.

The amidation reaction of EDA with terminal methyl ester groups of GQDs-PAMAM-G0.5 was carried out as follows: GQDs-PAMAM-G0.5 was dispersed in anhydrous methanol by using the ultrasonic bath. The reaction temperature was adjusted at 50 °C. Subsequently, under the argon atmosphere, EDA added dropwise into the mixture having GQDs-PAMAM-G0.5. After 24 h, the resulting GQDs-PAMAM-G1 collected by using a centrifuge at 10,000 rpm and then washed with anhydrous ethanol repetitively. After purification of the final product via the repeating washing and centrifuging operations, the obtained powder dried in a vacuum oven for further use. To propagate each generation of the GQDs-PAMAM dendrimer, each of the Michael addition and the amidation reactions was alternated and repeated. The amounts of the used reagents in each step are listed in Table S1.

For the synthesis of GQDs-PAMAM-β-CD glycodendrimer, mono-6-OTs-β-CD (1 g) and GQDs-PAMAM-G3 (0.5 g) were added to the reaction balloon having methanol (60 mL). The reaction mixture was stirred under argon environment at 60 °C for 48 h. The prepared GQDs-PAMAM-β-CD glycodendrimer was collected with centrifuge and then washed with methanol several times. The obtained precipitate was dried at 50 °C in a vacuum oven for 48 h (Guo & Qin, 2014).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Calculation the amount of the grafted β-CD to the GQDs-PAMAM
ce:section.ce:para:
To determine the amount of the β-CD in the GQDs-PAMAM-β-CD, 10 mg of GQDs-PAMAM-β-CD was refluxed in 15 mL of concentrated sulfuric acid (0.5 mol/L) at 100 °C. The obtained solution diluted to 50 mL by using the sulfuric acid (0.5 mol/L). 1.25 mL of the diluted solution mixed with 0.75 mL of phenol 5% (w/v), then the concentrated sulfuric acid (4.5 mL) was added to it. The spectrum of the solution was recorded and the absorbance at 490 nm used to determine the amount of the grafted β-CD. The β-CD content (%) was calculated using the Eq. 1 (A standard curve of glucose) and Eq. 2.
(1)
C
g l u c o s e
=
0.301
A
490 n m
+ 0.015
(2)
C D
%
=
C
g l u c o s e
× 50
m L
×
M
β - C D
7 ×
M
g l u c o s e
×
m
n a n o c o m p o s i t e
× 100 %
In the above equations C, Mglucose, Mβ-CD, and mnanocomposite respectively are the concentration of glucose (mg/mL), the molecular weight of glucose (180 Da), the molecular weight of β-CD (1135 Da), and the weight of used GQDs-PAMAM-β-CD (mg) for the doing of the test.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro DOX load and release study
ce:section.ce:para: The details of the DOX load and release are provided in Part 1.4 of ESI.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study of the drug release mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: The in vitro drug release kinetics are available in Part 1.5 of ESI.
Experimental details of the MTT assay are presented in Part 1.6.1 of ESI.
Cellular uptake test was performed by using both fluorescent microscopy and flow cytometry. Details are accessible in Part 1.6.2 of ESI.
The details of the DAPI staining assay can be found in Part 1.6.3 of ESI.
Experimental details of the cell cycle test are expressed in Part 1.6.4 of ESI.

The successful synthesis of GQDs-PAMAM-β-CD glycodendrimer was investigated by using the FT-IR analysis and the achieved spectra are shown in Fig. 1. GQDs exhibited the stretching vibration of the −OH and aromatic −CH groups at 3248 cm−1 and 3031 cm−1 respectively. The main peaks at 1394 cm−1, 1122 cm−1, and 1600–1800 cm−1 are known as the characteristic peaks of the symmetric stretching vibrations of −COO-, stretching vibration of CO, and the merged asymmetric stretching vibration of −COO− with stretching vibration of CC (Chaicham, Tuntulani, Promarak, & Tomapatanaget, 2019; Maity et al., 2015). Silylation of GQDs with APTS led to the appearance of the new peaks at 2941 cm−1 and 2850 cm−1 that are related to the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibration of −CH. Moreover, in this spectrum, the peaks at 1132 cm−1 and 1069 cm−1 assigned to the stretching vibration of the Si-O-Si and Si-O-C (Verma, Aila, Kaul, & Jain, 2014). The FT-IR spectrum of the different generation of dendrimer (G1, G2, and G3) shows the wide peak of the merged NH with CO, stretching vibration of C–H, and NH at 1550-1650 cm −1, 2900 cm−1, and 3400 cm−1 respectively. The appearance of these peaks and the enhancement in the C–H peaks intensity with an increase in the generation number could be a sign for the step by step growth of the PAMAM from the modified GQDs surface (Li et al., 2015). The successful functionalization of GQDs-PAMAM with β-CD oligosaccharide evaluated with the FT-IR technique. Mono-6-OTs-β-CD showed the main peaks at 2920 cm−1, 1589 cm−1, 1414 cm−1, and 1157 cm−1 that they are related to the stretching vibration of −CH, CC of the benzene ring, asymmetric stretching vibration of SO2, and symmetric vibration of SO2 (Pooresmaeil & Namazi, 2018b). The FT-IR spectrum of GQDs-PAMAM-β-CD is a math combination of the GQDs-PAMAM and mono-6-OTs-β-CD which could be a good reason for success in the functionalization reaction. The obtained results from the FT-IR indicate the successful growth of the PAMAM dendrimer from the modified GQDs surface and its functionalization with β-CD molecules.

Zeta potential analysis was used to determine the surface charges of the samples. Fig. 2 a reports the obtained values of the zeta potential for the samples. GQDs dispersion showed the surface overall charges of about -18.2 mV. The negative surface charges of GQDs could be due to the presence of oxygen-containing functional groups on it which deprotonated in the neutral medium. After modification with APTS and growth of the dendrimers, the zeta potentials were obtained about -6.5 mV, -5.2 mV, -4.0 mV, and -2.4 mV respectively. An increase in the obtained values with an increase in the generation number shows the successful growth of the different generations of PAMAM from the GQDs surface. With increasing the generation number, the amino group density increase, and as a result the zeta potential shifts to the higher value. After functionalization with β-CD, the zeta potential decreased to the -11.1 mV. This decrease could be related to the reaction of the free amine functional group s with β-CD.

The phenol-sulfuric acid test is a commonly used technique for the quantitative colorimetric determination of the oligosaccharide and polysaccharide content of the prepared samples. Hence, the amount of β-CD in the prepared glycodendrimer was determined by using the phenol-sulfuric acid test (Fig. 2b). The results of the test discovered that the glycodendrimer having β-CD (30.37 %) was successfully prepared. In fact, this test displayed the functionalization with β-CD as qualitative and obtained its value as quantitative.

The specific surface area, porosity, and the mean pore size of the drug carrier is the main parameters that affect its capability as a carrier. BET analysis was performed at 77 K and the obtained nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms and Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) plot are shown in Fig. 2c. The specific surface area, total pore volume, and mean pore size diameter of GQDs-PAMAM-β-CD calculated about 6.4074 m2 g−1, 0.0315 cm3 g−1, and 19.679 nm respectively that is higher than the reported value for the GQDs and it may result from the growth of dendrimer from the GQDs surface and its functionalization with β-CD. (Masteri-Farahani, Mashhadi-Ramezani, & Mosleh, 2019; Mehrdad-Vahdati et al., 2019). By considering the obtained results and the size of the DOX, it is expected that the prepared system could be a valuable nanocarrier for DOX delivery (Bilalis, Tziveleka, Varlas, & Iatrou, 2016). To further explore the properties of the GQDs-PAMAM-β-CD glycodendrimer, the PL spectrum was obtained to evaluate the potential of the prepared GQDs-PAMAM-β-CD glycodendrimer as a bioimaging nanodevice. The emission spectra for GQDs and GQDs-PAMAM-β-CD were recorded after excitation at 365 nm by using a UV lamp and the obtained spectra are displayed in Fig. 2d. The prepared GQDs showed blue photoluminescence as intense emission peak around 460 nm. After surface modification of GQDs and growth of the dendrimer from its surface, PL analysis showed a broad spectrum for GQDs-PAMAM-β-CD with a shift in emission wavelength. The observed changes could be due to photoluminescence reabsorption and partial quenching phenomena, as well as, the difference in the size and emission sites of two samples could be other reasons for the observed shift. Based on the PL analysis it is visible that the prepared system has the photoluminescent activity and could be used as a potential imaging agent (Javanbakht & Shaabani, 2019).

The particle size and morphology of the prepared GQDs and GQDs-PAMAM-β-CD glycodendrimer investigated by using the SEM analysis to study the change in the morphology and size of GQDs as the result of the modification. As can be seen from Fig. 3 , the GQDs showed a uniform spherical morphology with an average size of ∼5 nm. After the growth of the dendrimer from the GQDs surface, spherical morphology relatively was maintained but the size was increased; ∼ 55 nm. An increase in the diameter could be due to the modification of GQDs with APTS, dendrimer growth, and functionalization with β-CD. Moreover, it should be mentioned that the synthesized GQDs are well separated and aggregation was not found for them, however, after the modification, a low degree of aggregation was found which could result from the interaction between the chains.

Atomic force microscopy was used to calculate the typical thickness of GQDs before and after the modification and the respective results are shown in Fig. 4 . The investigation of the AFM images with the corresponding height profile shows that the GQDs-PAMAM-β-CD glycodendrimer has a relatively rougher surface in comparison with GQDs. The GQDs-PAMAM-β-CD obtained more height than GQDs. Compared to GQDs, the increase in height profile of GQDs-PAMAM-β-CD is apparently attributed to the modification with APTS, growth of dendrimer, and finally functionalization with the β-CD oligosaccharide. The results of AFM showed an agreement with SEM outcomes and show the successful modification of GQDs.

For further evaluation of the obtained results from the SEM and AFM analysis, the morphology of the GQDs and GQDs-PAMAM-β-CD was observed by using the TEM instrument to further clarify the morphology of the prepared samples. The obtained TEM images are shown in Fig. 5 a-b. TEM analysis discloses that the prepared GQDs were dispersed in a highly regular structure with a spherical shape. In the case of GQDs-PAMAM-β-CD, the obtained TEM images showed that the modifications increased the size of the particle. Overall, the TEM, SEM, and AFM results are in a line that describes the performed modification reactions were successfully performed on the GQDs surface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro drug load and release studies
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, DOX was selected to study the ability of the GQDs-PAMAM-β-CD glycodendrimer as an anticancer drug delivery nanodevice. Drug loading capacity and drug loading efficiency were calculated by using the Eq. S1 and Eq. S2 and obtained about 50.47 % and 5.05 % for GQDs-PAMAM, as well as, about 6.12 % and 61.20 % for GQDs-PAMAM-β-CD respectively. For both of the systems, the observed values could be due to the electrostatic interaction (pKa of DOX is 8.3), hydrogen bond-forming, and π–π interaction between the polar group of the DOX and nanocarrier. Additionally, the presence of a large number of the cavity in the nanocarrier structure offers the suitable sites for capturing of DOX in theirs. The comparison of the obtained value shows that the functionalization with β-CD increased drug loading percentage. This increase could be attributed to introducing the β-CD molecule with the ability for the formation of the host-guest interactions, as well as, its more polar functional groups. The in vitro DOX release behavior investigated for the prepared glycodendrimer in two different conditions. As shown in Fig. 6 a DOX@GQDs-PAMAM-β-CD showed time-dependent and a similar profile of DOX release in both pH 5.0 and pH 7.4. However, the amount of the released drug in pH 5.0 is higher than pH 7.4. This behavior shows the controlled drug release ability of the nanodevice and it is valuable in cancer therapy. The DOX@GQDs-PAMAM-β-CD released about 73.87 % and 24.50 % of its loaded drug at pH 5.0 and pH 7.4 respectively. The difference in the observed amount may result from several phenomena. First, in acidic conditions the formed π–π stacking interaction between DOX and aromatic network of GQDs-PAMAM-β-CD failed, hence, the more amount of drug released. Secondly, the DOX solubility in the acidic condition is higher; therefore, more DOX will be released from the DOX@GQDs-PAMAM-β-CD in this condition. Finally, hydrogen-bonding interaction between GQDs-PAMAM-β-CD and DOX in pH 7.4 is strong but it weakened in an acidic medium that leads to more releases of DOX in acidic conditions. The in-vitro drug delivery test showed that the GQDs-PAMAM-β-CD glycodendrimer potentially could be proposed for efficient DOX loading and its pH-sensitive delivery.

Four kinds of kinetic models were used to describe the drug release mechanism from the DOX@GQDs-PAMAM-β-CD. The obtained plots are given in Fig. S3-S4 and the outcomes are reported in Table 1 . The study of the drug release kinetics displayed that in both pHs the DOX release is well fitted with the Higuchi model with a high value of the coefficient of correlation (R2) in comparison with other models. Furthermore, the obtained value for “n” indicated that DOX released by the Fickian mechanism in pH 7.4 and non-Fickian mechanism in pH 5.0.

MDA-MB 231 was selected to perform the MTT test on the prepared samples. Fig. S5 shows the photo of the seeded cells in one well of the 96 well plate that the MDA-MB 231 cells were cultured in it and incubated for 24 h at 37 °C. From Fig. S5 it is visible that the MDA-MB 231 cells have spindle-shaped morphology and the required confluency reached after 24 h incubation of the cultured cells in the incubator. The in vitro cell viability assay was used to further explore the efficiency of the GQDs-PAMAM-β-CD, DOX, and DOX@GQDs-PAMAM-β-CD for breast cancer treatment (Fig. 6b-6d). MTT test showed 69 % and 65 % of cell viability after 48 h incubation with GQDs-PAMAM-β-CD and GQDs-PAMAM respectively. The observed results are in good accordance with (Shelat, Chandra, & Khanna, 2018). Therefore, it can be concluded that the functionalization of GQDs-PAMAM with β-CD improved its cell biocompatibility. Moreover, the MTT test performed to assess the cancer-killing efficacy of drug-loaded nanodevice. The results exhibit that in the same concentration of DOX, the viability of the cells after treatment with DOX@GQDs-PAMAM-β-CD is lower than DOX. This observation may be related to the difference in the mechanism of the drug penetration to the cancer cells and indicates that DOX could be efficiently carried to the cells by prepared nanocarrier. IC50 (half-maximal inhibitory concentration) which usually used for study the therapeutic effects of drugs, was obtained about 0.28 μg/mLand 0.2 μg/mL for DOX@GQDs-PAMAM-β-CD and DOX respectively (See Fig. 6d) and shows the more tumor cell killing ability for DOX@GQDs-PAMAM-β-CD in comparison with DOX. In fact, drug carriers with the nano architect could enter by endocytosis into cells in a one-way pathway while the free drugs are effluxed out by the membrane from the cell.

The cellular uptake is one of the main factors that exhibit the potential of the designed carrier for cancer treatment. Therefore, after treatment the cells with rhodamine B labeled samples the cellular uptake of the GQDs-PAMAM-β-CD and DOX@GQDs-PAMAM-β-CD was evaluated by using the fluorescence microscopy and flow cytometry. Fig. 7 exhibits the microscopy images of the treated cell after 3 h incubation with rhodamine B marked samples. The observed strong red fluorescence in the nucleus region and cytoplasm, signifying that the free or DOX-loaded GQDs-PAMAM-β-CD were entered into the cells through the endocytosis or diffusion mechanism. From the images, it is showable that the cell internalization efficiency is favorable after 3 h incubation. To further confirmation of the obtained results from the fluorescent microscopy, the percentage of the cell uptake was explored by using the flow cytometer after 3 h incubation. The quantitative cell uptake test elucidated the 100 % uptake after 3 h for both of the GQDs-PAMAM-β-CD and DOX@GQDs-PAMAM-β-CD which are in good accordance with fluorescent microscopy imaging results and is valuable in drug delivery.

Investigation of the morphology of chromatin and the cell population is an efficient tool for understanding the healthy, necrotic, and apoptotic of the cells. Hence, in the present work, the cells were treated with GQDs-PAMAM-β-CD, DOX, and DOX@GQDs-PAMAM-β-CD. After staining with DAPI and 48 h incubation, microscopic images of the cells were obtained. As shown in Fig. 8 , the stained cells showed the blue fluorescence. The treated cells with GQDs-PAMAM-β-CD showed a similar morphology with the control group and didn’t exhibit significant chromatin degradation. However, treatment with both of the DOX and DOX@GQDs-PAMAM-β-CD induced chromatin fragmentation. Additionally, more of the cells were dead as a result of the treatment with them, therefore, the cell population is low (See Fig. 8b-c). The observed chromatin fragmentation for the treated cells with DOX@GQDs-PAMAM-β-CD is more than DOX which approved the MTT results and efficiency of the prepared system as a drug carrier.

For further verifying of the obtained results from the MTT, cell uptake, and DAPI, the cycles of MDA-MB 231 cells after treatment with GQDs-PAMAM-β-CD, DOX, and DOX@GQDs-PAMAM-β-CD were analyzed by using the flow cytometry. The obtained cell cycles for each sample with the population (%) of the cells in each phase are displayed in Fig. 9 . The cell cycle pattern of the treated cells with GQDs-PAMAM-β-CD is the same as the control that shows its biocompatibility. However, treatment with DOX and DOX@GQDs-PAMAM-β-CD led to arresting the cell cycle in sub G1 and S phases. The more efficiency of the DOX@GQDs-PAMAM-β-CD in comparison with free DOX is understandable with its more apoptosis effect.

In summary, in this work, the growth of the new PAMAM dendrimer having terminal β-CD groups as a drug carrier from the surface of the modified GQDs was presented. In this regard, at first, GQDs-PAMAM was synthesized in several steps. Because of the problems in the case of the PAMAM dendrimers, GQDs-PAMAM functionalized with biocompatible β-CD. FT-IR, zeta potential, SEM, TEM, and AFM analysis clarified the dendrimer growth. Additionally, the PL analysis approved the inherent fluorescent activity of the GQDs-PAMAM-β-CD. 62.1 % of DOX was loaded in the prepared glycodendrimer. DOX@GQDs-PAMAM-β-CD displayed pH-controlled drug releases over a prolonged time. The safety of the GQDs-PAMAM-β-CD can be ascribed from the MTT (cell viability about 69 %), DAPI, and cell cycle tests. MTT, cell uptake, DAPI, and cell cycle techniques exhibited the better antitumor efficacy of DOX@GQDs-PAMAM-β-CD compare to free DOX via its more killing, chromatin fragmentation, and apoptosis effects. Based on the outcomes of this work, it could be proposed that the GQDs-PAMAM-β-CD glycodendrimer could be proposed as an efficient drug delivery system with the potential of its monitoring.

Malihe Pooresmaeil carried out the experiment and wrote the manuscript with support from Hassan Namazi. Hassan Namazi supervised the project. All authors discussed the results and contributed to the final manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that there are no conflicts of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors gratefully acknowledge the University of Tabriz (grant number 948645103) and Research Center for Pharmaceutical Nanotechnology (RCPN) of the Tabriz University of Medical Science for the financial supports of this research.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbpol.2020.116658.
